verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: How Athanasius acted when this took place.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Now why did they form this plot against me, and treacherously lay an ambush to take me, when it was in their power to enforce the order by a written declaration?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: The command of an Emperor is wont to give great boldness to those entrusted with it; but their desire to act secretly made the suspicion stronger that they had received no command.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And did I require anything so very absurd?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Will not every one say, that such a demand was reasonable for a Bishop to make?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: You know, for you have read the Scriptures, how great an offence it is for a Bishop to desert his Church, and to neglect the flocks of God.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: For the absence of the Shepherd gives the wolves an opportunity to attack the sheep.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And this was what the Arians and all the other heretics desired, that during my absence they might find an opportunity to entrap the people into impiety.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: If then I had fled, what defence could I have made before the true Bishops? or rather before Him Who has committed to me His flock?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: He it is Who judges the whole earth, the true King of all, our Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Would not every one have rightly charged me with neglect of my people?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Would not your Piety have blamed me, and have justly asked, ‘After you had returned under the authority of our letters, why did you withdraw without such authority, and desert your people?’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Would not the people themselves at the day of judgment have reasonably imputed to me this neglect of them, and have said, ‘He that had the oversight of us fled, and we were neglected, there being no one to put us in mind of our duty?’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: When they said this, what could I have answered?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Such a complaint was made by Ezekiel against the Pastors of old; and the blessed Apostle Paul, knowing this, has charged every one of us through his disciple, saying, ‘Neglect not the gift that is in thee, which was given thee, with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery.’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Fearing this, I wished not to flee, but to receive your commands, if indeed such was the will of your Piety.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But I never obtained what I so reasonably requested, and now I am falsely accused before you; for I resisted no commands of your Piety; nor will I now attempt to return to Alexandria, until your Grace shall desire it.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: This I say beforehand, lest the slanderers should again make this a pretence for accusing me.
